text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['President', 'Department of Commerce', 'Trump administration'], topics=['section 232', 'copper', 'tariffs', 'national security', 'trade investigation'].

## BACKGROUND (12)
- [2025-02-10] President Donald J. Trump signed proclamations on February 10, 2025, reinstating 25% tariffs on all steel imports and increasing tariffs on aluminum imports from 10% to 25%. The actions, taken under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, terminated prior alternative agreements that had provided tariff relief to countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, Japan, Mexico, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom. These countries will now be subject to the full 25% tariff.

Effective March 12, 2025, all steel, steel derivative products, and aluminum products previously covered by Section 232 will be subject to a 25% tariff. The proclamations also imposed an additional 25% ad valorem duty on certain new derivative steel and aluminum articles. The Commerce Secretary's authority to grant new product exclusion requests or renew existing ones was eliminated, effective immediately. A narrow exclusion was created for derivative steel and aluminum products originating from U.S.-melted or smelted articles. By May 11, 2025, the Commerce Secretary must establish a process for U.S. producers to request the inclusion of additional derivative products under these tariffs.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump delivered his second inaugural address, outlining plans for executive orders related to deportations, energy, and tariffs.

On the same day, the President issued a memorandum stating that prior administration commitments regarding the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Global Tax Deal have no force or effect in the United States unless adopted by Congress. The memorandum directed the Treasury Secretary and the U.S. Permanent Representative to the OECD to formally notify the organization of this position. It also instructed the Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the United States Trade Representative, to investigate foreign countries for non-compliance with tax treaties or for implementing extraterritorial tax rules on American companies, requiring a list of responsive actions within 60 days.

Additionally, the administration issued Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” directing federal agencies to immediately pause the disbursement of funds appropriated through the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This has resulted in slowed funding for various programs and subsequent litigation.
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issued executive orders, memoranda, and proclamations. Actions included a presidential proclamation declaring an "invasion" at the southern border, suspending migrant entry and asylum requests. Executive Order 14165 "Securing Our Borders" reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" program, restarted border wall construction, and limited humanitarian parole. A national emergency was declared at the border, directing military deployment. An executive order also sought to reinterpret the 14th Amendment regarding birthright citizenship, facing immediate legal challenges.

In energy and environmental policy, Executive Order 14154 "Unleashing American Energy" promoted fossil fuel production and critical mineral development, and ended federal support for electric vehicles. The U.S. withdrew from the Paris Agreement and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. A national energy emergency was declared to expedite fossil fuel projects.

Regarding federal operations, a hiring freeze was implemented across most executive branch agencies. A "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" was enacted for new rules. Federal employees were directed to return to in-person work. An executive order defined sex as an immutable biological classification, directing agencies to remove materials promoting "gender ideology." The administration also established an "America First Trade Policy" and revoked 58 executive orders and 10 presidential memoranda from the previous administration, targeting diversity, equity, and inclusion policies.
- [2025-03-12] U.S. President Donald Trump's 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports took effect on Wednesday, March 12, 2025, following a White House confirmation on Tuesday. The duties apply to imports from nations including Canada, Australia, and the European Union.

The European Union announced it would impose counter-tariffs totaling 26 billion euros ($28.33 billion) on U.S. goods starting in April. These EU measures include re-imposing 8 billion euros in previously suspended tariffs and implementing new countermeasures on 18 billion euros of goods.

Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese criticized Trump's tariffs but stated Australia would not impose reciprocal tariffs. The White House also confirmed Trump would not raise tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum imports to 50%, a decision linked to Ontario Premier Doug Ford pausing a 25% tax on electricity exports to the U.S.
- [2025-03-04] The Trump administration imposed additional 10% tariffs on all Chinese goods on March 3, 2025, under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. On March 4, the administration implemented new tariffs, increasing duties on Chinese imports from 10% to 20%. Imports from Canada and Mexico faced an additional 25% duty, with Canadian energy resources and critical minerals subject to a 10% tariff. Products qualifying under USMCA from Canada and Mexico were largely excluded. Global steel and aluminum imports will face an extra 25% tariff starting March 12, 2025. Imports qualifying for de minimis entry from Canada, Mexico, and China were temporarily exempt.

China announced retaliatory trade measures on March 4, including new 10-15% tariffs on U.S. agricultural products, effective March 10. China's General Administration of Customs suspended export authorization for three U.S. soybean producers and halted imports of all U.S. logs. China also added 10 U.S. companies to its Unreliable Entities List and 15 U.S. companies to its Control List, imposing various prohibitions and restrictions. Canada and China announced plans for reciprocal tariffs to take effect April 2, 2025, including 25% additional tariffs on lumber and copper.
- [2025-02-01] U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order on February 1, 2025, imposing new tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico, and China. The order mandates a 25% ad valorem duty on most Canadian goods and a 10% ad valorem duty on Canadian energy products, effective 12:01 a.m. ET on February 4, 2025. Trump cited the sustained influx of illicit drugs, including fentanyl, and illegal border crossings as reasons for the action, expanding a previously declared national emergency. The order invoked authorities under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act (NEA, specifying that duty-free de minimis treatment would not apply to affected articles. In response, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced 25% retaliatory tariffs against $155 billion worth of American goods. Canada's immediate response will target $30 billion worth of goods starting February 4, with an additional $125 billion in tariffs to follow in 21 days. Canadian officials disputed the U.S. claims regarding fentanyl and migrant origins. Premiers from several Canadian provinces expressed support for the federal government's retaliatory measures.
- [2025-02-10] On February 10, 2025, President Donald Trump announced new 25% ad valorem tariffs on imported steel articles, aluminum articles, and their derivatives, set to take effect on March 12, 2025. These actions, implemented under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, increase tariffs to 25% for all covered products, eliminate all country and general exclusions, and prohibit new product exclusion requests. Existing product exclusions will terminate by their end date or when quotas are reached. Previous alternative agreements with countries including Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, Mexico, the European Union, Japan, South Korea, Ukraine, and the United Kingdom are deemed ineffective. New derivative articles like fabricated structural steel will be added, and the Department of Commerce will establish a process for identifying additional derivatives. Customs and Border Protection will prioritize inspections and apply maximum penalties for duty evasion. Formal Presidential Proclamations detailing the in-scope articles were released on February 14, 2025.
- [2025-02-10] On February 10, 2025, President Donald Trump issued two proclamations under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962: 'Adjusting Imports of Steel into the United States' and 'Adjusting Imports of Aluminum into the United States.' The proclamations expanded existing tariffs, citing increased imports, global excess capacity, and national security.

For steel, the proclamations restored a 25% ad valorem tariff on imports from Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada, Mexico, South Korea, the European Union, Japan, the United Kingdom, and Ukraine. The tariffs also applied to additional steel derivatives.

For aluminum, the ad valorem tariff increased from 10% to 25%. Agreements with Argentina, Australia, Canada, Mexico, the European Union, and the United Kingdom were terminated.

The tariff changes became effective for products entered for consumption on or after March 12, 2025. Both proclamations also terminated the Section 232 exclusion process as of February 10, 2025, at 11:59 p.m. EST.
- [2025-02-01] President Donald J. Trump issued an order on February 1, 2025, imposing an additional 25 percent ad valorem duty on all articles that are products of Mexico. The duties are scheduled to apply to goods entered for consumption on or after 12:01 a.m. eastern time on February 4, 2025. The action is taken under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the National Emergencies Act (NEA), expanding the scope of a national emergency declared on January 20, 2025 (Proclamation 10886). Trump stated that Mexico's failure to intercept drug trafficking organizations, human traffickers, and illicit drugs constitutes an "unusual and extraordinary threat" to U.S. national security, foreign policy, and economy. The Secretary of Homeland Security is directed to make necessary modifications to the Harmonized Tariff Schedule of the United States (HTSUS) and to consult with other officials. The order states the tariffs can be removed if the government of Mexico takes adequate steps to alleviate illegal migration and illicit drug issues. The order also states that the President may increase or expand the duties if Mexico retaliates.
- [2025-02-10] On February 10, 2025, President Donald Trump signed an executive order imposing a 25% tariff on all steel and aluminum imports into the United States, effective March 12, 2025. The order eliminates all previous country exemptions, reinstating and expanding tariffs initially introduced in 2018. The policy affects trading partners including Canada, Mexico, Japan, South Korea, and the European Union. The tariffs now extend beyond raw materials to include downstream products such as fabricated structural steel and introduce a "Melted and Poured" rule for processing in the originating country. The U.S. Department of Commerce cited increased steel imports, increased steel exports from China, global overcapacity projected to reach 630 million metric tons by 2026, and national security concerns as reasons for the policy, stating it aims to maintain a domestic steel capacity utilization rate above 80%.
- [2025-02-13] President Donald J. Trump signed a Presidential Memorandum on February 13, 2025, ordering the development of a 'Fair and Reciprocal Plan' on trade. The plan's stated goal is to restore fairness in U.S. trade relationships and address non-reciprocal trading arrangements. The administration stated the plan's objectives include prioritizing American workers, improving industrial competitiveness, reducing the trade deficit, and bolstering economic and national security. The memorandum cited examples of non-reciprocal trade practices, including higher tariffs imposed by countries such as Brazil, India, and the European Union on U.S. exports, compared to lower U.S. tariffs on their goods. It also noted bans on U.S. products, such as EU restrictions on shellfish, and digital service taxes levied by nations like Canada and France on American companies. The U.S. recorded a trade deficit in goods exceeding $1 trillion in 2024, and an agricultural trade deficit of approximately $40 billion in the same year.
- [2025-03-12] The Trump administration imposed 25% ad valorem tariffs on all steel and aluminum articles and their derivatives, effective March 12, 2025, at 12:01 a.m. Aluminum articles and derivatives from Russia are subject to 200% tariffs. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) guidance confirmed reporting requirements for "melted and poured" / "smelted and cast" products. All existing tariff rate quotas and general/country exclusions were terminated as of March 11, 2025, and no new product exclusion requests are currently being processed. Canada announced 25% retaliatory tariffs on approximately $21 billion worth of U.S. goods, effective March 13, 2025, citing violations of USMCA and WTO obligations. The European Union outlined a two-step approach, ending the suspension of existing countermeasures on April 1, 2025, and introducing new countermeasures by mid-April 2025. Mexico indicated a potential delay in its retaliatory measures until April 2, 2025.
